annotations.label: Primitive recursive function (informal)

anchor.snippet: a function that can be computed by a computer program whose loops are all

annotations.note: Mathlib's inductive `Nat.Primrec` (zero, succ, projections, composition, primitive recursion) is exactly the formal counterpart of this informal class.

annotations.label: Strict subset of total general recursive functions

anchor.snippet: form a strict subset of those general recursive functions that are also total functions

annotations.note: `Primrec.to_comp` gives the inclusion into total computable functions and Ackermann results witness strictness, but no single decl states the strict-subset proposition.

annotations.label: Time-complexity bound criterion

anchor.snippet: it suffices to show that its time complexity is bounded above by a primitive recursive function

annotations.note: No formalization of a time-complexity-based criterion for primitive recursiveness exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Primitive recursive function arity

anchor.snippet: takes a fixed number of arguments

annotations.note: `Nat.Primrec'` is the textbook n-ary definition over `Vector ℕ n`, directly encoding fixed-arity primitive recursive functions.

annotations.label: Constant functions (basic)

anchor.snippet: the k -ary constant function

annotations.note: `Nat.Primrec'.const` proves the n-ary constant function is primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Successor function (basic)

anchor.snippet: which returns the successor of its argument

annotations.note: The successor is a primitive constructor `succ` in both `Nat.Primrec` and `Nat.Primrec'`.

annotations.label: Projection functions (basic)

anchor.snippet: For all natural numbers

annotations.note: The projection constructor `Nat.Primrec'.get i` formalizes the basic n-ary projection functions.

annotations.label: Composition operator

anchor.snippet: also called the substitution operator

annotations.note: `Nat.Primrec'.comp` (and `Nat.Primrec.comp`/`Primrec.comp`) is exactly the composition/substitution closure operator.

annotations.label: Primitive recursion operator

anchor.snippet: acts as a for-loop from

annotations.note: `Nat.Primrec'.prec` is the primitive-recursion constructor matching this for-loop operator.

anchors.value: {\displaystyle {\begin{aligned}\rho (g,h)&\ {\stackrel {\mathrm {def} }{=}}\ f,\quad {\text{where the }}(k+1){\text{-ary function }}f{\text{ is defined by}}\\f(y,x_{1},\dots ,x_{k})&={\begin{cases}g(x_{1},\dots ,x_{k})&{\text{if }}y=0,\\h(y',f(y',x_{1},\dots ,x_{k}),x_{1},\dots ,x_{k})&{\text{if }}y=S(y'){\text{ for a }}y'\in \mathbb {N} .\end{cases}}\end{aligned}}}

annotations.label: Class of primitive recursive functions

anchor.snippet: are the basic functions and those obtained from the basic functions

annotations.note: `Nat.Primrec'` is the inductively-defined least class closed under the basic functions and operators, with `prim_iff` linking it to `Nat.Primrec`.

annotations.label: Vector-valued primitive recursive function

anchor.snippet: is primitive recursive if it can be written as

annotations.note: `Nat.Primrec'.Vec` defines a vector-valued function to be primitive recursive iff all of its component projections are.

annotations.label: Constant unary function example

anchor.snippet: is a 1-ary function which returns

annotations.note: `Primrec.const` shows any constant function (including the unary case) is primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Zero-ary constant example

anchor.snippet: is a 0-ary function, i.e. a constant

annotations.note: `Nat.Primrec'.zero` is precisely the 0-ary constant zero function.

annotations.label: Identity function example

anchor.snippet: is the identity function on the natural numbers

annotations.note: `Primrec.id` (and `Nat.Primrec.id`) proves the identity function is primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Left and right projection example

anchor.snippet: is the left and right projection on natural number pairs

annotations.note: `Primrec.fst` and `Primrec.snd` give the left and right projections as primitive recursive functions.

annotations.label: Add-2 function example

anchor.snippet: is a 1-ary function that adds 2 to its input

annotations.note: Not stated as a named lemma, but it is the immediate composition `succ ∘ succ` of the primitive recursive `Primrec.succ`.

annotations.label: Constants as compositions

anchor.snippet: are the same function

annotations.note: This is an informal remark about two notations denoting the same constant function, with no formal counterpart.

annotations.label: Addition function

anchor.snippet: to compute the sum of its arguments

annotations.note: `Nat.Primrec.add` (also `Primrec.nat_add`, `Nat.Primrec'.add`) proves addition is primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Doubling function

anchor.snippet: doubles its argument

annotations.note: `Primrec.nat_double` proves `fun n => 2 * n` is primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Multiplication function

anchor.snippet: multiplication can be defined by

annotations.note: `Nat.Primrec.mul` (also `Primrec.nat_mul`, `Nat.Primrec'.mul`) proves multiplication is primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Predecessor function

anchor.snippet: acts as the "opposite" of the successor function

annotations.note: `Nat.Primrec.pred` (also `Primrec.pred`, `Nat.Primrec'.pred`) proves the predecessor is primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Truncated subtraction (monus)

anchor.snippet: is definable from the predecessor function

annotations.note: `Nat.Primrec.sub` (also `Primrec.nat_sub`, `Nat.Primrec'.sub`) proves truncated subtraction is primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Identifying predicates with numeric functions

anchor.snippet: identifying the truth values with numbers in any fixed manner

annotations.note: `PrimrecPred p` is defined as `decide ∘ p` being primitive recursive, i.e. identifying a predicate with its Boolean/numeric characteristic function.

annotations.label: Is-zero predicate

anchor.snippet: As an example for a primitive recursive predicate

annotations.note: The is-zero predicate is the instance `Primrec.eq` composed with the constant `0`, giving a `PrimrecPred`.

annotations.label: Less-or-equal predicate

anchor.snippet: Using the property

annotations.note: `Primrec.nat_le : PrimrecRel (· ≤ ·)` proves `≤` on ℕ is a primitive recursive relation.

annotations.label: Greater-or-equal predicate

anchor.snippet: the converse predicate can be defined as

annotations.note: The converse `≥` is `Primrec.nat_le` swapped via `PrimrecRel.swap`.

annotations.label: If-then-else operator

anchor.snippet: known from programming languages can be defined by

annotations.note: `Primrec.cond` (and `Primrec.ite`) formalizes the primitive recursive if-then-else operator.

annotations.label: Logical conjunction

anchor.snippet: logical conjunction of

annotations.note: `PrimrecPred.and` (and `Primrec.and` on Bool) proves conjunction of primitive recursive predicates is primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Disjunction and negation

anchor.snippet: lead to appropriate definitions of disjunction and negation

annotations.note: `PrimrecPred.or` and `PrimrecPred.not` (with `Primrec.or`/`Primrec.not`) formalize disjunction and negation.

annotations.label: Equality predicate

anchor.snippet: implements the equality predicate

annotations.note: `Primrec.eq : PrimrecRel (@Eq α)` (and `Primrec.beq`) proves equality is a primitive recursive relation.

annotations.label: Less-than and greater-than predicates

anchor.snippet: implements the predicate "less-than"

annotations.note: `Primrec.nat_lt : PrimrecRel (· < ·)` proves `<`, with greater-than obtained by swapping.

annotations.label: Exponentiation and primality are primitive recursive

anchor.snippet: Exponentiation and primality testing are primitive recursive

annotations.note: Exponentiation is formalized as `Nat.Primrec.pow`, but no primitive recursive primality test is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Case function is primitive recursive

anchor.snippet: a function that returns the value of

annotations.note: `Primrec.nat_casesOn` (and `Nat.Primrec.casesOn'`) formalizes the primitive recursive case/branching function.

annotations.label: Extension via Gödel numberings

anchor.section: Operations on integers and rational numbers
anchor.snippet: can be extended to operate on other objects such as integers and rational numbers

annotations.note: The `Primcodable` type class is exactly Mathlib's Gödel-numbering mechanism for extending primitive recursion to encodable types.

annotations.label: Integer arithmetic operations primitive recursive

anchor.section: Operations on integers and rational numbers
anchor.snippet: the arithmetic operations including addition, subtraction, and multiplication are all primitive recursive

annotations.note: Mathlib provides `Primcodable ℤ` and the `Primrec` framework but does not prove integer +, −, × are primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Rational field operations primitive recursive

anchor.section: Operations on integers and rational numbers
anchor.snippet: the field operations are all primitive recursive

annotations.note: No proofs that the rational field operations are primitive recursive exist in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exponentiation

anchor.snippet: Exponentiation: a

annotations.note: `Nat.Primrec.pow` proves `fun (a,b) => a ^ b` is primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Factorial

anchor.snippet: Factorial a!

annotations.note: No proof that `Nat.factorial` is primitive recursive is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Predecessor / decrement

anchor.snippet: Predecessor or decrement

annotations.note: `Nat.Primrec.pred` proves the predecessor/decrement is primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Proper subtraction

anchor.snippet: Proper subtraction

annotations.note: `Nat.Primrec.sub` (truncated/proper subtraction on ℕ) is proved primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Minimum and Maximum

anchor.snippet: Minimum(a

annotations.note: `Primrec.nat_min` and `Primrec.nat_max` prove min and max on ℕ are primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Absolute difference

anchor.snippet: Absolute difference

annotations.note: Not a named lemma, but expressible from the primitive recursive `Primrec.nat_sub`/`nat_max` (e.g. `(a-b)+(b-a)`).

annotations.label: Signum and its negation

anchor.snippet: NOT[signum(a)]

annotations.note: No dedicated signum lemma, but it is the immediate case-split `if n = 0 then 0 else 1` built from `Primrec.nat_casesOn`/`Primrec.eq`.

annotations.label: Divisibility

anchor.snippet: a divides b

annotations.note: No primitive recursive divisibility predicate is stated, but it follows from `Primrec.nat_mod` being zero (`b % a = 0`).

annotations.label: Remainder / MOD

anchor.snippet: the leftover if b does not divide a

annotations.note: `Primrec.nat_mod` proves `(· % ·)` on ℕ is primitive recursive (via `Primrec.nat_div`).

annotations.label: Primality predicate

anchor.snippet: a is a prime number

annotations.note: Mathlib does not prove `Nat.Prime` is a primitive recursive predicate.

annotations.label: i+1th prime

anchor.snippet: the i+1th prime number

annotations.note: No proof that the prime-enumeration function is primitive recursive exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exponent of prime in number

anchor.snippet: exponent of p

annotations.note: No primitive recursive prime-exponent (Gödel decoding) function is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Length (number of nonzero exponents)

anchor.snippet: non-vanishing exponents

annotations.note: No primitive recursive 'length of Gödel encoding' function is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Integer logarithm

anchor.snippet: logarithm of a to base b

annotations.note: `Nat.log` exists but is not proven primitive recursive in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Explicit definition closure (#A)

anchor.snippet: definable explicitly from functions

annotations.note: Closure under explicit definition/substitution is the `Primrec.comp` family of lemmas.

annotations.label: Bounded sum and product (#B)

anchor.snippet: The finite sum

annotations.note: No named bounded-sum/product lemma exists, but `Primrec.list_foldl` with `Primrec.list_range` provides the infrastructure to build them.

annotations.label: Substitution into predicate (#C)

anchor.snippet: obtained by substituting functions

annotations.note: `PrimrecPred.comp` and `PrimrecRel.comp` formalize substitution of primitive recursive functions into primitive recursive predicates.

annotations.label: Logical connectives of predicates (#D)

anchor.snippet: The following predicates are primitive recursive in Q and R

annotations.note: `PrimrecPred.and`/`.or`/`.not` show the class of primitive recursive predicates is closed under the logical connectives.

annotations.label: Bounded quantifiers and minimization (#E)

anchor.snippet: The following predicates are primitive recursive in the predicate R

annotations.note: `PrimrecPred.exists_lt`/`forall_lt` (and `PrimrecRel` versions) formalize bounded existential/universal quantifiers as primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Bounded mu-operator

anchor.snippet: a bounded form of the so-called minimization- or mu-operator

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Primrec.nat_findGreatest` (bounded maximization) but no dedicated bounded least-witness μ-operator lemma.

annotations.label: Definition by cases (#F)

anchor.snippet: Definition by cases

annotations.note: `Primrec.cond`/`Primrec.ite`/`Primrec.nat_casesOn` formalize definition by cases.

annotations.label: Course-of-values recursion (#G)

anchor.snippet: the course-of-values function encodes the sequence of values

annotations.note: `Primrec.nat_strong_rec` formalizes course-of-values (strong) recursion producing primitive recursive functions.

annotations.label: Partial recursive function

anchor.snippet: is defined by introducing an unbounded search operator

annotations.note: `Nat.Partrec`/`Partrec` adds the unbounded-search `rfind` (μ) operator, matching the definition of partial recursive functions.

annotations.label: Turing-machine characterization of partial recursive

anchor.snippet: An equivalent definition states that a partial recursive function

annotations.note: `Turing.PartrecToTM2.tr_eval` shows a Turing machine evaluates any partial recursive code, but the full bi-directional equivalence is not packaged as a single iff.

annotations.label: Total recursive function

anchor.snippet: is a partial recursive function that is defined for every input

annotations.note: `Computable f` is defined as `Partrec (↑f)`, i.e. a partial recursive function total on every input.

annotations.label: Primitive recursive implies total recursive

anchor.snippet: Every primitive recursive function is total recursive

annotations.note: `Primrec.to_comp` proves every primitive recursive function is computable (total recursive).

annotations.label: Ackermann function not primitive recursive

anchor.snippet: is a well-known example of a total recursive function

annotations.note: `not_primrec₂_ack` proves Ackermann is not primitive recursive while `computable₂_ack` proves it is computable (total recursive).

annotations.label: Ackermann-bound characterization

anchor.snippet: if and only if there is a natural number

annotations.note: `exists_lt_ack_of_nat_primrec` proves the forward bound (every primitive recursive `f` is dominated by some `ack m`), but the full iff characterization is not formalized.

annotations.label: Primitive recursive set is recursively enumerable

anchor.snippet: they are a recursively enumerable subset of the set of all total recursive functions

annotations.note: Mathlib has `REPred` but does not prove the set of primitive recursive functions is a recursively enumerable subset of the total recursive functions.

annotations.label: Encoding multi-arg as unary via pairing

anchor.snippet: can be encoded as unary primitive recursive functions by using a primitive recursive pairing function

annotations.note: `Nat.Primrec'.prim_iff` proves the n-ary definition is equivalent to the unary `Nat.Primrec` using the primitive recursive pairing function `Nat.pair`.

annotations.label: Enumerator f is not primitive recursive

anchor.snippet: is not recursive primitive in itself

annotations.note: The diagonal argument that an enumerator of primitive recursive functions is itself not primitive recursive is not formalized.

annotations.label: Not the largest r.e. subset

anchor.snippet: is not the largest recursively enumerable subset of the set of all total recursive functions

annotations.note: This proposition about r.e. subsets of total recursive functions is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Provably total but converse fails

anchor.snippet: all primitive recursive functions are provably total

annotations.note: This metamathematical provable-totality statement (and failure of its converse) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Not all total computable functions are primitive recursive

anchor.snippet: does not include every possible total computable function

annotations.note: Witnessed by combining `computable₂_ack` and `not_primrec₂_ack` (Ackermann is computable but not primitive recursive), though not stated as one theorem.

annotations.label: Evaluator function not primitive recursive

anchor.snippet: Now define the "evaluator function"

annotations.note: Mathlib has a computable universal evaluator (`Nat.Partrec.Code.eval`) but no proof that an evaluator of primitive recursive functions fails to be primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Diagonal Ackermann m to A(m,m)

anchor.snippet: is a unary total recursive function that is not primitive recursive

annotations.note: `not_primrec_ack_self` proves the diagonal `fun n => ack n n` is not primitive recursive (it is computable via `computable₂_ack`).

annotations.label: Paris–Harrington function

anchor.snippet: The Paris–Harrington theorem involves a total recursive function

annotations.note: The Paris–Harrington theorem and its associated function are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sudan function

anchor.snippet: The Sudan function

annotations.note: The Sudan function is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Goodstein function

anchor.snippet: The Goodstein function

annotations.note: No Goodstein function or Goodstein's theorem is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Single zero function alternative

anchor.snippet: alternative definitions use just one 0-ary zero function

annotations.note: Mathlib uses a single 0-ary `Nat.Primrec'.zero` and derives all constants in `Nat.Primrec'.const`, but the equivalence of definitional variants is not stated as a theorem.

annotations.label: Iteration rule

anchor.snippet: the so-called iteration rule

annotations.note: `Primrec.nat_iterate` formalizes that iterating a primitive recursive function is primitive recursive.

annotations.label: Iteration rule generates all PR functions

anchor.snippet: the class of all primitive recursive functions can still be obtained in this way

annotations.note: Mathlib uses primitive recursion as primitive; there is no theorem that the iteration rule alone generates all primitive recursive functions.

annotations.label: Pure recursion rule

anchor.snippet: pure recursion , where h does not have access to the induction variable

annotations.note: The 'pure recursion' rule (step function without access to the induction variable) is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pure recursion suffices (Gladstone)

anchor.snippet: this rule is enough to generate all primitive recursive functions

annotations.note: Gladstone's theorem that pure recursion generates all primitive recursive functions is not formalized.

annotations.label: Pure iteration rule suffices

anchor.snippet: the pure iteration rule below, is enough

annotations.note: No theorem that the pure iteration rule suffices to generate all primitive recursive functions exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pure iteration without parameters (Severin)

anchor.snippet: the pure iteration rule without parameters

annotations.note: Severin's result on parameter-free pure iteration is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Course-of-values recursion gives PR functions

anchor.snippet: Course-of-values recursion defines primitive recursive functions

annotations.note: `Primrec.nat_strong_rec` proves course-of-values recursion yields primitive recursive functions.

annotations.label: Mutual recursion gives PR functions

anchor.snippet: Some forms of mutual recursion also define primitive recursive functions

annotations.note: `Primrec.nat_omega_rec` (well-founded ω-recursion) covers related recursion schemes, but mutual recursion is not formalized as such.

annotations.label: LOOP language equals PR functions

anchor.snippet: are exactly the primitive recursive functions

annotations.note: The LOOP programming language and its equivalence with the primitive recursive functions are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Primitive recursive programming language

anchor.snippet: is one that contains basic arithmetic operators

annotations.note: No primitive-recursive programming language is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: LOOP computing power

anchor.snippet: Its computing power coincides with the primitive recursive functions

annotations.note: No theorem relating a LOOP language's computing power to the primitive recursive functions exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: PR computation always halts

anchor.snippet: implies that their computation halts on every input

annotations.note: Totality is implicit in primitive recursive functions being total `ℕ → ℕ` functions, with `Primrec.to_comp` placing them among the computable functions, but halting is not stated as a theorem.

annotations.label: Halting problem undecidable for general recursive

anchor.snippet: the halting problem is undecidable for general recursive functions

annotations.note: `ComputablePred.halting_problem` proves the halting problem is not computable (undecidable).

annotations.label: Primitive recursive arithmetic (PRA)

anchor.snippet: a formal axiom system for the natural numbers and the primitive recursive functions

annotations.note: The PRA axiom system is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: PRA weaker than Peano arithmetic

anchor.snippet: PRA is much weaker than Peano arithmetic

annotations.note: No comparison of proof-theoretic strength between PRA and Peano arithmetic is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gödel incompleteness formalized in PRA

anchor.snippet: then PRA proves the implication

annotations.note: Gödel's incompleteness theorems are not formalized in Mathlib, let alone within PRA.

annotations.label: PR functions carry out syntactic transformations

anchor.snippet: there are primitive recursive functions that carry out the corresponding syntactic transformations

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize primitive recursive functions performing proof-theoretic syntactic transformations.

annotations.label: Finitistic consistency proof

anchor.snippet: consistency proofs that themselves are finitistically acceptable

annotations.note: The notion of a finitistically acceptable consistency proof is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sufficient condition for finitistic proof

anchor.snippet: One sufficient condition for a consistency proof to be finitistic

annotations.note: No sufficient condition for finitistic consistency proofs is formalized in Mathlib.
